data.title: The AI Wealth Transfer No One Sees Coming - And Why I'm Preparing Now
data.description:
Introduction

The AI story is about to become more complex, way more complex.

This is one of the articles that took me many days to write, as we are not only dealing with AI, which is already an important topic itself due to its innovative disruption and its importance in the top-heavy S&P 500 (see below), but also because we’re increasingly uncovering new layers that have implications for investors, the market, and the economy.

In this article, I’ll cover at least two very important aspects of the AI revolution that are related to each other:

  1. We are seeing increasing funding issues as the AI trend is shifting to the next growth stage.
  2. This has implications for future market drivers and places to generate alpha.

So, as we truly have a lot to discuss, let’s keep this intro short for once and get right to it!

You Don't Know It Yet, But You’re Funding It

Initially, I wanted to start this article a bit differently, by asking you if you knew you were going to pay for the data center buildout. That would have been a catchy way to start.

The Wall Street Journal asked the same question in the title of a recent article: "Who Will Pay for the AI Revolution? Retirees."

By now, I doubt that it may come as a surprise that the AI data center buildout is expensive. Many others and I have brought this up so much that it’s almost mainstream news at this point. According to JPMorgan data, the biggest five hyperscalers alone are expected to spend more than $210 billion on CapEx this year. By 2027, that number could be close to $550 billion. Again, that’s just five private companies. Think about that for a second.

The Wall Street Journal used data from Morgan Stanley, which estimates that the global data center buildout needs roughly $3.0 trillion through 2028. This number alone may seem high, but it doesn’t tell the full story. It gets really interesting if we include the bank’s assumption that only half of this will be funded by expected cash flows. In other words, the operating cash flows from the hyperscalers I just mentioned and their peers.

A quick calculation shows that this gives us a $1.5 trillion funding gap. Again, that’s through 2028, which means this gap needs to be filled within three years if we assume hyperscalers won’t accept delays for innovation and competition purposes.

So, where does that money come from?

It’s coming from the bond market.

The WSJ notes that JPMorgan expects the bond market to absorb $300 billion of debt related to data centers next year.

Again, so far, so good.

The problem is that the high-grade corporate bond market, which only the best companies can access, isn’t really known for overwhelming its investors with massive long-term projects. Usually, it’s for bonds that mature within a decade and cover smaller deals, like M&A and whatnot. Now, it suddenly needs to deal with trillions in long-term demand from data centers alone.

That’s where retirees and other insurance-type companies, which look for returns, have long time horizons and lots of capital to deploy:

In their investments in recent years, insurers have been willing to turn to less-vanilla instruments. A research analysis published by the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago found that life insurers had been investing more in “higher yielding, but more complex, private placements.”

So with ready potential buyers that can stomach additional complexity or duration in exchange for more yield and size, even in the investment-grade market, more offerings to fund AI’s build-out seem likely. - WSJ

Is this a problem?

It depends. The one thing that’s for sure is that the structure of the investment-grade market is changing due to the risks and the volumes (in demand) that come with the data center buildout.

This is the perfect bridge to what I’m going to say next.

The Hidden Risks Of AI Are Slowly Being Uncovered

When AI first became mainstream, everyone was simply focused on just how much potential this technology may have. Stocks like NVIDIA (NVDA) exploded because everyone knew the demand for chips would skyrocket.

That’s still the case to some extent.

The major difference is that we’re now increasingly focused on two other things:

  1. How big will the returns be of massive investments in AI?
  2. Where’s the money coming from?

We already briefly discussed the point above. The answer is external funding.

However, zooming in here is important, as not all AI players are the same. As we can see below, in 2022, companies like Alphabet (GOOGL) and Microsoft (MSFT) were almost drowning in cash, as cash and short-term investments accounted for almost 45% of their total assets. On a side note, that’s also why they were somewhat shielded against higher rates after the pandemic, as their cash investments started to generate higher returns.

Now, these cash buffers have shrunken a lot.

But that’s not everything. We aren’t simply dealing with one-off expenses, but a situation where changing technologies turn capital-light software companies into heavy CapEx spenders, with more cyclical risks and sensitive balance sheets.

While I am not making the case that these tech companies are now bad investments, it needs to be said that the environment is different. Not only are these companies increasingly competing with each other, but they are also forced to spend heavily on chips and infrastructure that likely come with steep depreciation.

Keeping this in mind, it’s leading to a rotation.

Think Of AI As An Entire Ecosystem

One of my favorite charts I’ve seen all month is the one below, which shows three types of ratios (vs. the S&P 500).

  • Infrastructure builders: Data centers, chip producers, and other hardware players.
  • AI revenue beneficiaries: Software and apps that sell AI products.
  • Productivity laggards: The companies that will eventually win as a result of AI adoption.

As we can see, infrastructure has absolutely crushed it, which may not come as a surprise, as it’s home to NVIDIA (NVDA) and other stocks that were winners from day one. AI revenue beneficiaries (enabled revenues) have outperformed the market since June 2023, with a sideways movement since the end of 2024. They have done quite well.

Productivity laggards have lagged the market, as they just made a relative new low compared to the Standard & Poor’s 500. As we have often discussed, this is no surprise, as the market is simply reliant on the infrastructure and software players.

I’m bringing this up for a number of reasons. One reason is that the massive CapEx plans we already discussed need to lead to high returns. After all, investors who provide capital are looking to get a return on their money. The same goes for investors in hyperscalers who want to see high returns on capital to justify hundreds of billions in long-term CapEx.

John Authers had a fantastic paragraph in an opinion piece earlier this week, as he found that data centers need to generate $2 trillion in annual revenue by 2030. Currently, that number is $20 billion. This would translate to a boost of 100x. Hundred(!).

As for infrastructure stocks that are building data centers, they’ll need to transform their massive investments into real returns. Bain & Co. estimates that to justify their cost, these data centers will need to generate $2 trillion in annual revenue by 2030. With current revenues of roughly $20 billion, Kai Wu of Sparkline Capital notes that the business would have to expand a hundredfold. - Via Bloomberg

In light of these numbers, it’s no surprise that some compare this to the telecom spending of the late-1990s, when companies like AT&T (T) spent hundreds of billions on fiber-optic cables, which turned out to be way too much in light of the actual payout that followed.

Currently, demand doesn’t seem to be an issue, as Oracle (ORCL) made very clear during its latest earnings call:

Okay. So as I mentioned before, most people are assuming that we are demand constrained, but we're not. We're actually supply constrained. And the thing to understand here is that a large part of what I spend my time doing is securing what I consider to be good supply. I'll give you an example of an e-mail I received recently. There was a very nice person who lives in Nebraska, who has a corn field.

They said that -- they think that would be a really good place to put an AI data center. You're probably laughing, but I received. So there's -- you cannot imagine the amount of reach outs that come my way in the way of my team.

It's actually quite a hard job to filter through all of the opportunities to understand which ones bring together the energy that you need, the hard work, the capacity that you need, the land that you need and the capital that you need. Our job is to put together all of those pieces. And only then does that result in supply. - ORCL 3Q25 Earnings Call

The only thing that’s keeping them from innovating and earning is supply bottlenecks.

Nonetheless, we still don't really know where earnings will come from, which is why these infrastructure stocks have been doing so well. Going back to the John Authers article, there are three reasons for that:

  • We simply don’t know where the ROIs will show up.
  • Infrastructure has been concentrated in a few key players that all have pricing power.
  • Many AI beneficiaries could be private companies.

Here, I would add that we simply don’t know what companies will benefit most, as we cannot guess just how powerful and widely adopted AI will become. I believe uncertainty is a huge factor.

Two Critical Phases & The Next Potential Winners

So far, we have discussed a few things I consider to be extremely important for the AI revolution and the market, in general.

I am now convinced this is leading to a new market environment and a number of phases.

  • Phase 1. I believe this is the phase we’re currently leaving. Hyperscalers and related infrastructure companies started funding the AI boom. They were the first ones that saw real economic benefits and enjoyed massive investor demand. In most cases, I think it was fully deserved, as America is truly home to the best AI players, as the past 2-3 years have shown.
  • Phase 2. I think we just entered this phase. It’s the phase where we really start to realize just how expensive this data center buildout is. This is leading to a much bigger role of the debt market, as hyperscalers are "sharing" the risks. These hyperscalers are now also much more CapEx-heavy, meaning on top of having to fight for AI leadership, they are not the hyperefficient companies they used to be. This also means that on top of sharing risks, profits need to be shared, as both equity and debt investors require a return on their investment. This is especially important, as large insurance companies and other organizations are involved in these funding operations.

Essentially, Phase 1 was all about building the foundation of AI. After all, we basically went from no AI to having to build the foundation in just a few short years. Now, the next phase is about proving that there’s an actual economic benefit here.

Hence, I think Phase 2 will see a major capital rotation. I think this could take more than a decade and will see capital flows from initial infrastructure winners with higher capital misallocation risks to the companies that can adopt AI to become more efficient.

In other words, it’s all about AI benefits going mainstream.

I believe this will be a slow process in 2026 and maybe 2027. However, once adoption grows and AI shows superintelligence, these benefits will rapidly work their way through the economy.

Needless to say, this does not mean the Mag-7 is dead money. All I am saying is that the risk/reward is changing. We also need to see this through the lens of a long-term investor. What happens today, tomorrow, or next month on the stock market is irrelevant for this thesis. This is all about longer-term cash flows and beneficiaries.

So, what kind of companies can benefit?

It is super tough to explain which companies will be the best AI adopters. For starters, I don’t know every single stock-listed company well enough to judge their ability to adapt and implement these strategies. Nobody does. Also, we don’t know just how powerful AI will become. Just think of the possibilities. We could get tangible AI through humanoids that operate entire automated factories, allowing America to compete with ultra-cheap labor overseas. Think about what this could mean for economic reshoring(!).

There are a few things I would keep in mind when picking stocks for phase 2:

  • Companies that have structurally low margins due to high labor or material costs can benefit. These can be companies that are very process-heavy. In other words, not your asset-light software companies that consist of a few people in an office.
  • When the companies from the bullet point above are "data rich," AI adoption can be easier. For example, Realty Income (O) owns more than 15,000 properties. I can only imagine how much data it collects on a daily basis. Or think of a health insurance company like UnitedHealth Group (UNH).

In my own portfolio, I have a number of companies I expect to be big AI winners over time. This includes my three transportation companies, Union Pacific (UNP), Canadian Pacific (CP), and Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL). The first two are Class I railroads. ODFL is a less-than-truckload company that could see the biggest benefits out of all of them.

They can schedule freight more efficiently, reduce downtime, enhance utilization rates, get more customer data, and maybe even predict the conditions on the road, future demand, and so much more. I also expect ODFL to automate its distribution centers and even work with driverless trucks. However, this also means that ODFL could see more competition, as building an efficient LTL network (which is the company’s moat) isn’t as hard as it was in the past.

Another group of winners is distribution companies. This includes my investment in QXO (QXO), which buys housing (mainly roofing) supplies, stores them, and sells them to buyers like contractors. AI could improve the predictability of demand and supply, enhance warehousing, improve delivery, and so much more. Other winners could be Lineage (LINE) and AmeriCold (COLD), which are cold-storage REITs with high yields, distribution giants like Pool Corp. (POOL) and Watsco (WSO), as well as giants like PepsiCo (PEP) that have massive global supply chains.

Other companies include agriculture giants like Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM), which owns countless distribution and processing facilities, industrial suppliers like Fastenal (FAST), and healthcare companies like CVS Health (CVS), which has a huge retail business and insurance platform. Just think of fraud detection, benefit approval, and other benefits.

Other companies I really like are Waste Management (WM), Republic Services (RSG), and Waste Connections (WCN), which have very CapEx-intensive business models. AI adoption could streamline them from A to Z.

The same likely goes for Home Depot (HD), Costco (COST), and Walmart (WMT), which shows just how widespread AI adoption benefits can become. That’s one of the reasons why the market’s lofty valuation isn’t necessarily a concern.

Then there’s Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) and other exchanges, which can generate data more easily, package it, and sell it to customers. In general, they can streamline operations and bring new products to market faster.

Needless to say, I’ll spend a lot more time on this in the future, as there is way more to unpack.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

AI isn’t just complex, but also a story that is developing rapidly.

In this article, which has become one of my favorites this month, we discussed a potential transition to a new phase of AI investing. We’re slowly going into an environment where AI investments need to pay off, which is putting more pressure on the hyperscalers and involves a lot more external funding.

This could be terrific for companies that can adopt AI to boost earnings. This would broaden strength beyond software, including data-rich companies with elevated CapEx and operating costs. Think of transportation, distributors, retailers, real estate, and many, many more.

My point isn’t that the Mag-7 group is dead, but that the alpha advantage is shifting, which could have a major impact on the top-heavy S&P 500 and the involvement of insurance companies.

This shifts AI benefits and creates new risks.

In other words, I think we’re looking at exciting times for macro research and stock picking.

data.title: American Tower: Well-Covered 4% Dividend Potentially Attractive For Income-Oriented Investors (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

At the time of writing, the market is down over 1%. Continuing its sell-off from last week, some stocks now trade at what I consider to be attractive valuations in comparison to their quality.

As a buy-and-hold investor, buying the dip on high-quality companies can lead to outperformance in the long run. In the case of American Tower Corp. (AMT), a cell-tower REIT that just reported solid Q3 earnings, their latest dip is one income-focused investors should consider.

In this article, I discuss American Tower's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why, despite their valuation below their historical average and solid yield, I'm downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

Previous Buy Thesis

It's been a while since I last covered American Tower, last assigning the REIT a buy back in April of 2024 in an article titled: Buy-And-Hold Investors Should Be Euphoric About This Bargain.

Since then, the stock has been somewhat volatile but is up 5.29% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up 33.30%. The REIT reached a new 52-week high back in July but has seen its share price pull back significantly.

During their then Q4 earnings, AMT missed bottom-line expectations, with AFFO missing by $0.03. However, this still managed to see solid growth with AFFO amounting to $2.29.

This was impacted by higher financing costs and FX. Revenue was $2.79 billion, a beat by $50 million. AMT's balance sheet, well-covered dividend with a payout ratio of 65%, forward P/AFFO of 16.6x well-below their historical average, and growth outlook were reasons I remained bullish.

Strong Quarter

American Tower reported their Q3 earnings at the end of October and delivered a strong report with a beat on both its top and bottom lines. AFFO of $2.78 beat by a noticeable $0.32, while revenue of $2.72 billion beat by $60 million.

As a result, the REIT raised their EBITDA outlook for 2025 as well as their top and bottom line estimates. This was a result of strong leasing and AI driving demand in their data center business. In fact, strong retail leasing led to a record quarter.

With the AFFO guidance increase to $10.60 - $10.72, this represents solid growth of 7% and is up from $10.46 - $10.65 prior. On a per share basis, AFFO increased 5.3%, while revenue grew 8% year-over-year.

So, despite AMT seeing increased volatility in recent months, the stock has continued to deliver solidly amidst uncertainty. And while this could continue, long-term I think the stock could be a compelling investment.

During earnings, management stated the demand for mobile data was strong. They mentioned how mobile data consumption last year grew 35% for the third consecutive year.

And this was due to growth in customers, devices, and usage per device. With mobile data being a huge part of AMT's business, the REIT only stands to benefit from this as companies will need more towers.

Management also stated that experts believe that mobile consumption will continue to double every 2 to 3 years and that network capacity will double over the next half decade.

Meaning AMT looks poised to benefit as the need for cell tower capacity will increase as a result. And this is evidenced by the planned buildout of an additional 2,150 towers linked to data center CAPEX.

American Tower also has a partnership with AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), a satellite designer that connects mobile devices through satellite usage.

Strong Financial Position

American Tower remains in a comfortable position financially from a balance sheet standpoint. Leverage during the quarter was a low 4.9x. This is lower than when I last covered them in early 2024 when their net debt to EBITDA was 5.2x.

Liquidity was also robust during the quarter, with $10.7 billion available and manageable debt maturing in the next few years. Most of this was also fixed-rate at 94%.

Another indicator of AMT's financial strength is the company's share repurchases. Subsequent to the quarter's end, management bought back $28 million worth of shares and had $2 billion remaining on their current program. With recent volatility, I expect these to pick up in the coming quarters.

Near 4% Dividend Yield

AMT's dividend yield close to 4% could also be considered attractive. Using their dividend estimate of $6.83, this gives them a well-covered dividend yield with a projected payout ratio of 64% using their guidance midpoint of $10.66. According to management, they expect to pay out $3.2 billion in dividends with $5 billion in AFFO.

Cell Tower REITs Have Possibly Re-Rated

From a valuation standpoint, American Tower Corp. appears attractive as they trade well below their historical average. Although I like the REIT long-term and from a dividend yield perspective, investors may not see significant upside, at least not for the near term.

According to F.A.S.T. Graphs, they have the REIT's normal P/AFFO multiple at close to 23.0x. At their current price, this gives them a forward multiple of 16.92x.

Although we're used to seeing cell tower REITs trade at normal multiples above 20x earnings, the market may have re-rated them since the pandemic in 2020.

You can also see AMT's previous year's growth in the F.A.S.T. Graphs chart above, where they experienced high-double-digit growth. But over the past 5 years or so, this has dwindled to mid single digits.

Looking at the chart below, all three cell-tower REITs have underperformed over the past 1, 3, and 5 years.

At least for the near to medium-term, I suspect AMT will trade in the 17x - 20x range. Using the midpoint, this gives American Tower Corp. a price target near $200, roughly 11% upside from the current price.

A potential long-term bullish signal on AMT is that REIT investment manager, AEW Capital Management recently exited various REIT positions and took a stake in AMT.

They could and are likely to see tailwinds from lower base rates and offer some hope for solid growth. But expecting the REIT to return to its normal multiple may be a long shot. As previously mentioned, I do think they remain attractive if you're looking for income and have a long-term time horizon.

Risks & Bottom Line

A risk for American Tower is that the stock could continue to see underperformance. Moreover, if the economy continues to see cracks, they could see a slowdown in deal flow and M&A activity.

American Tower Corp. could be subject to further downside, but I think the REIT is more likely to trade range-bound. And this could lead to near- to medium-term underperformance going forward.

From a valuation standpoint, despite appearing attractive, American Tower Corp. and peers have likely re-rated due to lower growth vs previous years. If you're an income-oriented investor, then the dividend yield close to 4% may be worth considering.

data.title: 12% Yields: Why Blackstone Secured Lending Beats Oaktree Specialty Lending
data.description:
Two business development companies (BIZD) that have recently pulled back despite being backed by world-class management companies in Blackstone (BX) and Oaktree, as well as having portfolios that consist primarily of senior secured loans, are Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) and Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL). In this article, I am going to compare them side-by-side after they recently reported their quarterly results and share why I think BXSL is slightly better today.

Portfolio Quality And Underwriting Comparison

Both funds are heavily concentrated in senior secured lending, but BXSL has been able to put up stronger underwriting numbers in recent years, proving itself to have a higher quality portfolio. On BXSL’s part, 98% of its portfolio is in first lien senior secured loans, with 99% of its investments being sponsor-backed. The average loan-to-value on its investments is 49.7%, with a weighted average interest coverage ratio of 2.0 times. Meanwhile, PIK comes in at 8.2% of total investment income, which is not particularly low, but it is not so high either as to cause major concern. This is especially true given that its non-accruals are very low at just 0.1% of both cost and fair value.

Meanwhile, OCSL has 83% of its portfolio invested in first lien senior secured loans. While BXSL’s weighted average borrower EBITDA is at $221 million, OCSL’s is slightly smaller at an average EBITDA of $150 million. That said, its weighted average interest coverage ratio on its loans is higher than BXSL’s at 2.2 times, and its PIK is lower at 5.3% of total income. However, its non-accruals have been quite elevated recently, especially compared to BXSL. Today, they sit at 5.9% at cost and 2.8% at fair value. Therefore, overall, BXSL’s portfolio comes across as much higher quality than OCSL’s. OCSL continues to work through some of its non-accrual positions in life sciences and healthcare, which are operationally challenging and taking quite a bit of time to resolve.

Balance Sheet And Liquidity

When it comes to the balance sheet, BXSL has considerably more leverage at 1.22 times, which is within its 1.0 to 1.25 times target range, while OCSL’s is much lower at 0.97 times on a net debt-to-equity ratio basis. Both have sufficient liquidity, as BXSL has $2.5 billion of liquidity, whereas OCSL has considerably less liquidity, but still more than a sufficient amount at $695 million.

One area where BXSL is also able to achieve higher returns on equity and better investment spreads is because its weighted average all-in cost of debt is just 5.04%, whereas OCSL’s is 6.5%. Both BXSL and OCSL are investment-grade rated, though one advantage that OCSL does have is that it has no debt maturing until 2027, with the vast majority of its debt not maturing until 2030. Meanwhile, BXSL has a substantial amount of debt maturing next year, all of which is fixed-rate at low-interest rates. This should therefore serve as a headwind for net investment income per share, as it has to refinance that, making its current very tight funding spread in the BDC space slightly overstated.

Dividend Sustainability And Coverage

When it comes to their current dividend yields, they are quite similar, as BXSL has an 11.71% yield and OCSL has a 12.02% dividend yield, both of which are considered base dividends. In terms of coverage of their dividends, BXSL currently has stronger coverage, as it generated $0.82 per share of net investment income in Q3, which covers its $0.77 per share dividend at a 1.06 times ratio. BXSL also has $1.89 per share of spillover income, which provides additional leeway for supporting the base dividend. Additionally, while its PIK is somewhat elevated, it is still below the peer average of 11.3%, and management has made clear on earnings calls that providing a stable base dividend is very important to them. Given the strength of their underlying portfolio, it is likely that they will pay out the base dividend as long as they can justify doing so from net investment income earnings as well as spillover income.

Meanwhile, OCSL has a much tighter dividend coverage ratio, with its latest quarterly net investment income coming in at $0.40, which is exactly in line with its base dividend at $0.40 per share. This is significant given that it already cut its base dividend by $0.15 earlier this year, from $0.55 to $0.40. That said, OCSL does have a big advantage in that its leverage ratio remains quite low at just 0.97 times, giving it substantial capacity to add additional leverage to further boost net investment income per share and provide better coverage of its dividend. Additionally, it has fewer balance sheet funding side headwinds than BXSL, which is another tailwind. Of course, the fact that its portfolio continues to have issues brings another level of risk to the equation that could force another dividend cut in the future if additional problems flare up.

Risk-Reward Trade-Off

Ultimately, both funds have highly reputable managers. Therefore, if you believe that OCSL’s issues are, as management calls them, one-time speed bumps that were due to their misreading of how quickly the COVID-19 pandemic would dissipate and conditions would normalize, then OCSL could be a very opportunistic bet here. If they can continue to improve their portfolio quality and underwriting performance, they can lean on adding additional leverage and their favorably structured balance sheet to drive additional net investment income. This would support the base dividend until eventually the market wakes up to the improved portfolio performance and thereby awards them with a higher multiple, all while paying investors an attractive 12% dividend yield. However, that intrinsically makes it riskier, whereas BXSL has fewer unknowns since it has had excellent underwriting performance ever since going public, and the significant capabilities that Blackstone brings to the table in support of BXSL make it a very attractive place to invest.

Investor Takeaway

Ultimately, I think both of these opportunities warrant a buy rating at their current discounts to NAV, but if I had to choose, and though I own neither of them today due to seeing other more attractive BDCs out there at the moment, if I had to pick one, I would pick BXSL. While OCSL’s discount to NAV is much wider than BXSL’s, BXSL still trades at a 3% discount to NAV, with much less uncertainty behind the investment thesis, given its much stronger underwriting performance recently. Additionally, the market has quite recently awarded BXSL a pretty substantial price-to-NAV premium, and it could quite easily return to that level if market sentiment turns on private credit.

Even just returning to a 10% premium to NAV could put BXSL back in the virtuous cycle of being able to issue equity above NAV at accretive levels and thereby grow net investment income per share more easily, which in turn would make its dividend quite sustainable.

Ultimately, I like OCSL as a deep-value opportunistic bet, whereas BXSL is an attractively valued, high-quality bet. In general, when choosing between a deep value recovery play and a high-quality company that is at a slightly undervalued price, I almost always will go with the high-quality play. Therefore, BXSL gets a slight edge here. Nevertheless, I am seeing other high-quality BDCs that are even more attractively priced than BXSL today. Therefore, I hold those instead and keep BXSL and OCSL on my watchlist for potential future inclusion in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Enviri Corporation Now Offers Tremendous Upside (Upgrade)
data.description:
November 21st was a fantastic day for shareholders of Enviri Corporation (NVRI). Shares of the company closed up 28.2% after management announced a major financial transaction that will completely restructure the business and create meaningful value for shareholders. In fact, I am astonished that shares did not rise drastically more than they did. In the past, I have been bullish about the company.

Back when it was known just as Harsco Corporation in January of 2023, I called it a ‘buy’ candidate. With this latest jump higher, shares are now up 117.9% compared to the 62.2% rise that the S&P 500 has enjoyed. But now that this new information is out, I view the company as offering far more upside than that. In fact, if things go according to plan, this could become one of the greatest opportunities for market participants, and the risk profile to me seems very low. I am so optimistic, in fact, that I have opened a small position in the stock. And that was specifically in relation to this development. Admittedly, it's only around 3% of my overall holdings. But I did that halfway through analyzing for this article. Now that I know the full ramifications of this monumental transaction, I am likely to significantly scale up my holdings. Naturally, this justifies upgrading the stock from a ‘buy’ to a ‘strong buy.

A great deal for shareholders

Before I get into the nature of the transaction, I think it would be helpful to provide a brief overview of what Enviri Corporation actually does. Conceptually, it is a pretty simple business. Management describes the firm as a provider of environmental solutions that caters to the industrial and specialty waste markets. It also produces certain equipment and technologies for the rail sector. To really understand the company, we need to dig a bit deeper. At this time, there are three separate operating segments.

The largest of these based on revenue is the Harsco Environmental segment, which operates as a provider of on-site environmental services and material processing capabilities for the global metals industry. Specifically, this business helps out the metal manufacturing process through the handling of waste and byproducts. It serves 70 million service customers spread across 130 sites that are situated in 30 different countries. Clearly, this is a global operation that deserves our attention.

The smallest of the firm’s segments would be its Harsco Rail segment. This part of the company produces certain rail equipment, such as surfacing equipment, rail treatment equipment, tie equipment, utility track vehicles, new track construction equipment, and more. About 40% of revenues for this segment, however, come from aftermarket parts and services, as well as safety and diagnostic technologies.

And then we have its Clean Earth business, which offers up specialty waste processing, treatment, recycling, and certain reuse solutions for customers in key markets like the industrial space, retail, healthcare, and construction. This is a rather intriguing business because it deals with a heavily regulated environment. But for any firm that can establish itself as a leader in this market, the rewards can be attractive. And with around 90,000 customer locations at its services using a fleet of around 800 vehicles, and with 700 important permits under its belt, it clearly fits that description.

In recent years, the financial performance for the business has been mixed. In the chart above, you can see revenue, profits, and cash flows, for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, as well as for the nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Cash flows for the business have been pretty much range-bound, though EBITDA has risen rather nicely. This year, however, conditions have been more challenging. Revenue during the first nine months of 2025 dropped to $1.69 billion compared to the $1.78 billion reported a year earlier. The firm's net loss widened significantly, while adjusted operating cash flow and EBITDA both took a heavy hit.

The pain for the company came primarily from its Harsco Environmental and Harsco Rail businesses. On the rail side of things, I will say that I am not surprised by the decline. As I've detailed in other articles about this specific industry, there has been a lot of pressure as of late. Broader economic conditions are worsening and as the US heads toward a recession, we can expect further weakening here. Management mentioned that the decline in revenue for this segment during the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year was driven primarily because of a net effect of price and volume changes, with management blaming this mostly on lower volumes as end market demand worsens.

More nuanced is the Harsco Environmental business, which saw revenue contract from $871.2 million to $762.2 million. With that decline came a plunge in operating profits from $73.1 million to only $27.6 million. There is some good news here. And this is that asset sales caused about $59.9 million of the sales decline during this window. This was a deliberate choice made by management, but that was not the only pain that it saw. A net loss in contracts and lower demand caused about $43.9 million in pain for the company. And, unfortunately, the firm saw some other issues, including a $10.3 million hit on the bottom line associated with its decision to exit a downstream business in France, plus asset sales negatively affected bottom-line results by $7.9 million, while lower demand for eco-products hit profitability by $4.9 million.

The only positive side of the business during this year has been the Clean Earth segment, with revenue popping from $698.9 million to $731.6 million. This caused a slight increase in operating income from $71.3 million to $74.1 million. The overall rise in revenue for the company was thanks to higher prices and a favorable change in volume, which is clear evidence that the picture for these operations is solid for the moment. It is this exact part of the pie, however, that management just made a big announcement about.

According to a press release issued on the morning of November 21st, management has decided to sell off Clean Earth to a company by the name of Veolia Environnement SA in a deal valued at $3.04 billion. To put the enormity of this in perspective, consider that, as of this writing, the total market capitalization of Enviri Corporation is $1.40 billion. Its enterprise value stands at $2.85 billion. In essence, management is selling part of the business for more than the entire company is worth. This is rare indeed.

This transaction will be a bit different than a traditional asset sale. At the time of the closing of the sale of Clean Earth, management will also engage in a spinoff transaction, whereby current shareholders of Enviri Corporation will receive 0.33 shares of this new business for each share of the company that they currently own. This structure will allow this transaction to be tax free, permitting the company to avoid substantially all of the taxes that they would otherwise have to pay on the sale of Clean Earth.

Management has some big plans as part of this. For starters, of the $3.04 billion that the company will rake in, it will pay $1.35 billion toward debt repayment. Considering that the net debt of the business right now is $1.41 billion, that will eliminate most of its debt on a net basis. However, this does not include $65 million of debt and debt-like obligations presumably associated with the sale of Clean Earth. There will be certain transaction related costs and adjustments ranging between $110 million and $150 million. And on top of this, the company plans to allocate between $60 million and $200 million toward their Harsco Rail segment’s engineered-to-order contracts. These are basically custom-built rail equipment, services, or systems, that are dedicated to specific customers. This has been a problem for the company because it has weighed materially on its Harsco Rail operation. But the good news is that management is phasing out this part of its business, solely because it is a loser for the company. So I view this as a one-time allocation.

After everything is said and done, we should be looking at between $1.275 billion and $1.455 billion in cash distributions that will then be paid out directly to shareholders. This translates to between $14.50 and $16.50 per share for a company that, after rising in response to this transaction, is worth $17.27. In essence, what investors are receiving is a lump sum of cash worth almost as much as the entire company, and they will also be receiving a debt-free business (sort of, as I will explain at the end) that is comprised of the other two operating segments I covered earlier.

This is the most excited I have been about a transaction in quite some time. Using estimates for 2025, management believes that the two remaining segments that investors are still going to own will generate annual revenue of about $1.27 billion. From it, the firm should generate around $136 million worth of EBITDA. If we take the midpoint of estimates for the cash distribution out of the current market capitalization of the company, we are looking at a total value for the firm of about $33 million. This is what the market is valuing a debt-free Harsco Environmental and Harsco Rail business at. This is nothing compared to the revenue and profitability this unit should bring in, as I detailed already.

Management pointed out a few different comparable firms for us to stack this new enterprise up against. In the chart above, you can see the net leverage ratio of these six companies. Once again, what remains of Enviri Corporation today should have essentially no debt. These companies, by comparison, have net leverage ratios ranging between 1.80 and 9.31.

In the next chart above, you can see the implied EV to EBITDA multiple of these six firms, while in the chart below that one, you can see the implied EV to revenue multiple of them. If we assume that Enviri Corporation should trade, after this transaction is completed in the middle of next year, at the low end of the range of these companies, we would be looking, as the chart below illustrates, at an upside of between 2,084.2% and 2,399.5%. And if we assume that it trades in the future at the average of these companies, stripping out the clear outlier that is TIC for the EV to EBITDA approach, upside would range between 3,065.1% and 5,450.3%. That is not a typo. And I had to go back and check the math a few times to make sure I wasn't hallucinating. But in essence, the market is assigning almost no value to what will remain. Admittedly, the rail business will probably see some additional pressures in the near term. But in the long run, both of these operations have an opportunity to do well. And even if they just perform at a mediocre level, it's not hard to see the stock doubling or tripling at least.

Obviously, upside could change based on management's own plans following the close of the transaction. They mentioned in their investor presentation, for instance, that the new business will have a net leverage ratio of 2. This seems to indicate that, even after paying off essentially all of their debt, they will take on new debt for the new enterprise amounting to approximately $272 million on a net basis. This could limit upside some. But as the chart below illustrates, we are still looking at massive upside even if this debt is used in some non-productive way.

Takeaway

The way I view it, this latest transaction offers significant potential for investors. I'm shocked we didn't see Enviri Corporation’s share price rise a lot more than it did. Assuming this transaction does go through, the payoff for shareholders should be massive. In response, I am upgrading the company from a ‘buy’ to a ‘strong buy,’ and I am very likely to buy up even more units and to potentially even purchase some call options in response to this development.

data.title: Salesforce: A Narrative Issue
data.description:
Salesforce (CRM) is an enterprise software company that provides solutions to companies workflows, with the aim of automating and streamlining day-to-day operations, which increases efficiency for enterprises. Salesforce continues to trade at lower prices, as growth rates as well as investor sentiment have been affected by the rise of AI, as one of the use cases of AI could perhaps make Salesforce's purpose obsolete. The enterprise workflow solutions companies as a whole continue to provide services, or at least try to provide services, that now include AI, in order for them to create value and higher productivity for workflows, such as Workday acquiring Paradox, which essentially uses AI to reshape the hiring process for companies. With the stock continuing to get beaten down as well as an unclear future, despite optimistic numbers for 2030, I would rather see results before being too optimistic, as AI seems to be a threat for many software companies, including Salesforce. Hence, I'm rating Salesforce at a 'Hold.'

What does Salesforce actually do?

Salesforce provides the full set of workflow solutions that a company demands, providing the technology for managing all of the company's relationships and interactions with customers and potential customers. Salesforce also offers a lot of functions that a company may need, such as their Data 360 (used to be called Data Cloud), analytics (Agentforce), and marketing.

Salesforce can also automate workflows like marketing workflows, as well as simply providing all a company needs when it comes to managing their own workflows, providing the software and system for them to thrive and increase productivity, rather than relying on manual work. With the rise of AI and agentic agents, Salesforce is now also transitioning to offering such services with Agentforce 360, which aims to provide a better experience to users.

With their own projections of over 40% of Fortune 1000 companies work being elevated by AI by 2029, according to their investor day 2025, as Salesforce seems to be bullish in the near future, but the stock price has spoken a whole different story in the recent years as the threat of AI towards Salesforce has created huge negative sentiment around the stock and underperformance compared to the S&P 500.

Thesis

Salesforce continues to be underappreciated in the market, underperforming on every single time scale up to 10 years. As Salesforce continues to get underappreciated, it continues to trade at a lower and lower premium, as Salesforce's stock continues to seem cheaper with every dip and decent earnings report.

I believe that the current narrative surrounding Salesforce is rather bearish, with AI potentially displacing Salesforce's value and essentially making it obsolete. Salesforce's core job of saving and running huge amounts of data is supposedly easily replaced by AI chatbots and LLMs, as LLMs are only getting better by the day. Using LLMs for workflows may be way better and cheaper than Salesforce. Salesforce's reply is Agentforce, but it is still at a relatively early stage, which hasn't yielded great results for Salesforce yet.

I believe it is now all about execution and changing people's narratives surrounding the company. From their investor day, they projected over $60B in revenue in 2030 alone, which makes their current valuations very cheap and attractive. However, until Salesforce is able to show the trend towards the bullish and optimistic number, markets and investors remain conservative, as Salesforce's stock price hasn't really moved on a 5 year scale. If Salesforce is able to execute well with Agentforce being widely adopted, Salesforce would trade at a higher premium, especially considering its current levels. Until the inflection point is clearer for Salesforce to trend upwards, I remain neutral on Salesforce, as their whole business model could be obsolete with the existence of AI chatbots and LLMs.

Salesforce's Stickiness

Enterprise workflow solutions software companies all tend to have a very similar moat, with its largest strength being high stickiness. When an enterprise integrates its operations into one of these workflows, so much data is integrated, from payroll, details on individuals and transactions. Due to this, moving data from one enterprise workflow to another would take time and would cause huge time lags as well as operations coming to a halt. Due to this, it simply wouldn't be rational for a firm to move over operations, considering operations would get affected, so the retention and renewal rate for these enterprise workflow platforms is high. There is even evidence now that many enterprises are unwilling to move over to newer platforms (SAP 4/HANA problem, which will be explored in the Concerns section), which shows that even a better, more advanced platform doesn't matter. Perhaps enterprises are too sticky, which could be a concern for these firms, as adoption of AI agents could be slower, unless the difference in productivity is so clear and very noticeable.

There isn't any recent data on the retention/renewal rate from Salesforce, with 2022's renewal rate around the 93% mark. From other workflow software companies like ServiceNow and Workday, their retention rates are at 98% and 97%, respectively, essentially showing that once their platforms are used, they essentially become embedded into a company's operation with huge stickiness.

Salesforce AI Potential

The next few quarters for Salesforce are very important, in order to highlight the progress of Agentforce and whether they are able to meet their 2030 projections with Agentforce being one of the key catalysts for their $60B revenue by FY30, which makes its current levels seem rather cheap. As seen above for the use cases, one of the use cases is to reduce time to first sale by up to 33%, increasing productivity overall.

Their business continues to grow with explosive growth rates, with their Data 360 and AI ARR at over $1B. With Agentic AI only at an ARR of $440M, it needs to continue to grow at such high rates to boost the narrative that AI won't displace Salesforce, but rather be able to provide even better services with AI that cannot be displaced for operations. Agentforce is still at a very speculative stage and far from being mature, which is a positive and a negative for investors. Salesforce has spent a huge amount on Agentforce, such as the acquisition of Informatica, which in their own recent press release when they completed the acquisition:

establishing a unified and comprehensive data foundation for agentic AI — and enabling AI agents to operate safely, responsibly, and at scale across the modern enterprise.

“You have to get your data right to get your AI right,” said Marc Benioff, Chair and CEO of Salesforce. “Data and context is the true fuel of Agentforce, and without clean, connected, trusted data there is no intelligence – only hallucination. Informatica is the trusted platform that turns fragmented enterprise data into context, so every agent can reason, act, and deliver outcomes with precision. When companies get their data right, they get their AI right, and Agentforce becomes unstoppable.”

Spending approximately $8B, it shows that Salesforce is truly committed to not being displaced by AI, but rather utilizing AI for the next step of the company. This obviously makes it very exciting for Salesforce's future, but with how the stock has reacted over the last year, it can be said that investors remain rather conservative on the future of Agentforce, considering chatbots could do what Agentforce does and potentially even better and cheaper. At its current price level and with an AI catalyst, I see the bullish thesis for Salesforce with Agentforce going smoothly and according to target, but there is certainly a bearish possibility where these enterprise workflow solution companies become obsolete, or at least have an alternative for enterprises that is potentially cheaper and better.

Most projected metrics for FY26 have been raised, while the projected GAAP EPS and operating margins are being cut. The investment towards AI is clearly taking a hit, and now the question is whether they can continue to grow to their targets whilst maintaining/expanding margins, which a better picture will be given next quarter. With the Informatica acquisition now closed, focus on questions in the next earnings call, which isn't too far away, as management could shed some more light on the integration of Informatica with Salesforce, and what it will turn out to be—essentially some form of roadmap for clarity to investors. I believe the next few quarters, starting from the next one, will be important for the trajectory of Salesforce, as any numbers on how Agentforce is doing and signs of potential acceleration will show investors that Agentforce can compete with chatbots in the future.

I believe that Salesforce is stuck in a narrative issue, which is honestly a common theme for many big names like Duolingo (AI displacement), Amazon (AWS slowdown), and Adobe (AI displacement) in the market right now, as indexes trade near all-time highs while these stocks are rather flat YTD or near 52-week lows. If Salesforce can get the narrative changing again, as it continues to trade at lower and lower premiums, Salesforce's stock could easily recover, but as long as it continues to have AI displacement fears, and Agentforce continues to be at a speculative/early stage, it is very likely that Salesforce will continue to be stagnant/decline.

Concerns

This is an idea that is in most of my articles on workflow enterprise solution companies, where the stickiness may perhaps be too sticky, so perhaps all their moats are to contain their customers and prevent them from leaving, which essentially leads to no one dominant firm in the industry. Since Salesforce's offerings don't really have a huge differentiation from other workflows, it is unlikely for Salesforce's core offerings to accelerate anytime soon, which means the stock's lower valuations may be justified since the explosive growth is already over, as Salesforce is too mature.

In more detail in my SAP article, 60% of SAP customers still use their legacy platform and have no intention of switching, as Gartner calls the switching deadline unrealistic. The original deadline was this year, with the deadline now at 2027.

This is a very odd issue that could also potentially reflect how well Agentforce might perform. The above issue is clearly a SAP one, but to me signals 2 things. First, the platform obviously has huge stickiness, so Salesforce is unlikely to get those enterprises business. Second, it is clear that users are content with the platform that has been provided, which raises the question: do enterprises need a better platform, or simply how many enterprises can utilize AI and actually find that it creates value or is more productive?

There is no doubt that Salesforce is trying to position itself for the future, but though we are rather early into this AI infrastructure buildout, understanding use cases of AI and the rapid improvement of LLMs, Salesforce is not ready now, but when it is ready in a few years, I have doubts that a chatbot wouldn't be able to replicate what Salesforce does, which essentially means there is a huge stickiness now, but perhaps not in the near future.

This essentially rolls into the concern that AI will displace Salesforce in the future, as instead of having full-on systems and software, LLMs will get so powerful that they will be able to accurately store data as well as provide data whenever the individual needs it. Rather than having systems on payroll and stuff, just typing in a simple command to show 'employee details' could essentially make Salesforce sort of obsolete. A lot of polishing will still be required, obviously, but I'm just trying to create the scenario with AI taking off and becoming perfect and enterprises increasing productivity with these chatbots, while Salesforce is in the list of one of the companies made obsolete by AI. I'm being rather bearish here, but with the stock's performance on every time scale up to 10 years, I believe that there is a good proportion of investors/analysts who believe this narrative/scenario as the stock continues to get beaten down.

Valuation

Salesforce has a current market cap of $222B, which, assuming it achieves its targets of $60B revenue by FY30, means that its P/S will be around 3.7 looking forward by over 4 years and 2 quarters.

With their historical P/S TTM at 7.52, if Salesforce trades at their historical averages by FY30 at this P/S assuming $60B in revenue, that would mean just over a double in stock price over 4 years and 2 months. That will likely outperform the S&P 500 at such rates, but the question now is how well they can actually execute. As their valuations across all metrics are trading considerably below historical averages by a significant margin, understandably due to the threat of AI, the question now is whether Salesforce can recover and overcome the AI problem or get beaten down by AI. This is why the next few quarters are so important, as Salesforce has set the stage for how it will perform over the coming years, so expectations for the first few quarters to be on its trajectory are very important for the future of Salesforce.

Salesforce now trades at the other lowest amongst some of its closest peers on a P/S basis, as the stock has lacked momentum in the last year. Salesforce does look cheap compared to the relative market as well as historical averages, but it is up to the investor to decide whether Salesforce can execute and utilize AI, or slowly become obsolete as LLMs can provide a way better service for cheaper. As I already mentioned, the stock needs a narrative change to indicate that it has a position in the future. Buying a piece of the company is believing that it will operate successfully in the future, as Salesforce's future is rather uncertain as of right now, which leads to lower premiums.

Final Thoughts

I'm rather bullish on AI and the use cases for LLMs in the future, so I'm slightly bearish on certain software companies like Salesforce, as I exited my position fully months back when Salesforce failed to recover after the April lows, while names in my portfolio like AMD recovered and recovered fast. Since then, there has been a narrative issue with Salesforce, as the stock has been stagnant and fluctuating around the $230s and $240s levels.

Valuations for Salesforce are rather cheap right now, as the future of Agentforce is certainly very exciting, but I'm staying away for now. I have already mentioned numerous times, and perhaps too many times, that Salesforce has a narrative issue. I'm not betting on the potential change of narrative, as this issue is most likely solved with one quarter of earnings showing that Agentforce has a place in the future, which could happen in any of the next quarters, especially now with Informatica, but I would rather stay on the sidelines than own Salesforce, to trade or to hold. If Salesforce continues not to change the narrative that AI could displace them and make them obsolete, it is likely that Salesforce will continue to trade like dead money, simply flat/trading lower.

data.title: Trying To Give A Credit Rating For OFS Credit Company
data.description:
In today's article, we will delve deeper into CLO CEFs. More specifically, we will try to step into Moody's shoes and give a credit rating to such a company that primarily invests in CLO equity securities. It is about OFS Credit Company Inc. (OCCI) - we will look at the fund and give it a credit rating, but not according to the CEFs methodology. You may wonder why, since OCCI is a CEF. While that is true, Moody's methodology is not designed for CLO CEFs, and the one that comes closest to our fund is the Moody's BDC methodology. Readers should take the credit scoring with a grain of salt. OCCI is not a BDC, and the credit agencies don't have a methodology to evaluate these CEFs. We are just seeing quite correlated moves in the BDC and CEF CLO space and want to make a simplified comparison.

OCCI Basic Info

We will briefly introduce you to the fund with a few basic details.

  • Asset under management: close to $148 million
  • Portfolio:

  • Industry exposure:

  • Holdings: The number of holdings is close to 75. Below are shown the top 10 of them:

  • Expense ratio: 12.43%
  • Non-leverage expense ratio: 8.2%
  • Management fees: 2.8%
  • Historical performance: The 5-year total return is close to 5%:

  • The common stock is currently trading at $4.51, with a close to 20% discount:

This chart is no joke. When a company loses 30% in a year, it cannot be viewed as a positive for any part of the capital structure. Something bad is happening.

Now let's get to the real part...

Credit rating for OCCI

Moody's considers six key factors in its BDC approach when evaluating the companies: Profitability, Asset Coverage Ratio Cushion, Asset Quality- Problem loans and Senior Secured loans, Cash flow and liquidity- Debt Structure and Secured debt. You can see them below in the picture:

Profitability

To assess profitability, Moody's uses a 3-year average net income-to-average-assets-under-management ratio. OCCI has approximately a negative 0.35% for this metric, so it has to take the 17 score on the Moody's scale, which is equivalent to a Caa1 credit rating.

Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio Cushion)

The regulatory limit for the asset coverage ratio for CEFs is 200%. The higher the coverage ratio, the better the credit score. The leverage of OCCI is around 43%. We calculated the asset coverage ratio cushion to be 17%. This is also one of the most important factors when assessing the company's overall credit score. The higher the ACRC, the better, as this simply means less leverage. The score we get here is 11, or a Ba1 equivalent. Keep in mind that we are using the regulatory limit for CEFs rather than BDCs, as the methodology states that other covenants may further reduce the asset coverage cushion.

Asset Quality

The company has no problem loans or senior secured debt, but here we will assign both metrics the worst credit rating on the Moody's scale, since OCCI mainly invests in CLO equity, which, as we know, is the riskiest tranche there is in the CLO securitization.

Cash flow and liquidity

OCCI has no secured debt whatsoever, which gives it another top score on the scale, or the equivalent of Aaa. This is the only decent score OCCI gets based on the methodology. Given that its assets generate high current income, OCCI mostly suffers from NAV deterioration rather than liquidity problems. This is why we give it an investment-grade liquidity score. Even a Ponzi scheme gets a high liquidity score at first, which is kind of funny.

Overall Score

The overall credit score of OCCI is equivalent to Ba1. But we also adjust the credit score based on market capitalization, premium to NAV, historical yield spreads, and management fees. Taking all these, OCCI gets a final score equivalent to a Ba3 credit rating:

I personally prefer a credit score that is better distributed among its constituents. When you have 3 categories below the Ca mark, the absence of secured debt fails to compensate. It is also quite important to note that at some point in the past, we were heavily invested in CEF CLO baby bonds and preferred stocks. One of the main reasons for doing so was the fact that the public used to treat the business model quite favorably, giving premium valuations to the common stocks. At that time, all our adjustments were lifting the credit score. We depended on our hedging reaction of shorting the common stock trading at an unjustified premium. Currently, this hedging reaction is gone, with the sector equity trading at double-digit discounts.

Conclusion

OCCI is a fund that invests primarily in CLO equity, and as such carries a higher risk than standard BDCs or corporate bonds. If we evaluate it according to Moody’s methodology for BDC companies, the overall score indicates Ba3, but at the lower end of that category, mainly due to the riskier assets in the portfolio. One can add their own understanding and evaluate credibility in a different way, resulting in a credit score that is much higher or much lower. Even within our team, our subjective assessments range from high investment grade to complete trash, depending on the person's bias.

data.title: SCHD: Stop Calling It A Value Fund; It's A Quality Momentum Strategy
data.description:
Thesis

Here's what bothers me about how most investors talk about the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). They call it a boring dividend fund or maybe a defensive value play. They’ll see its lower P/E ratio (~17.5x), focus on established companies, and stable yield (currently ~3.8%), and move on. This presents a comfortable, easy, but an incomplete view.

After analyzing how SCHD's underlying Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index (DJUSDIV) works, I've come to a different conclusion. SCHD is not designed to be a fixed basket of cheap, high-yielding stocks expected to compound slowly. Instead, it follows an index that uses an unemotional, rules based quantitative approach to identify high quality dividend paying companies with fundamental momentum.

I am not talking about price momentum here - that market obsession with stocks going up because they increased yesterday. I’m talking about Quality Momentum: the acceleration of actual business, profitability, and cash flow to shareholders.

The market is currently treating SCHD as a stagnant portfolio of aging value stocks, but in fact its portfolio represents the strong leaders of tomorrow.

What is the Quality Momentum Approach?

The simplicity of SCHD hides a very efficient quantitative process. Calling it a passive fund is unfair to the alpha it delivers. Let me walk you through the steps the index follows to select stocks with quality momentum.

Firstly, a company needs a track record of 10 years of dividend payments in a row before the index considers other measures like valuation, yield, or momentum. This does not just act as a dividend screen; it filters durability. This requirement instantly excludes recent IPOs, companies that suspended dividends during crises such as COVID-19, and those that focus only on growth rather than returns to shareholders.

What is left are businesses with solid cash flow and management discipline of paying back to shareholders across economic cycles.

The index then ranks these companies on dividend yield with an aim to select the top 100 based on 4 fundamental factors:

Cash Flow to Total Debt: Eliminates leverage risk and zombie companies

Return on Equity ("ROE"): Pure quality and capital efficiency metric

Dividend Yield: Ensures meaningful current income

5-Year Dividend Growth Rate: The secret sauce

This step filters for capital efficiency. It narrows the focus to companies that aren't just durable. These companies are generating so much extra cash that returning it to shareholders does not put any strain on the balance sheet.

The final measure (5-Yr Dividend Growth) is, in my view, the most important and misunderstood thing about SCHD. It is a lagging but very strong factor for selecting stocks whose fundamental momentum is accelerating.

Why is this important? Because a company cannot achieve high dividend growth without having recently experienced a sustained period of increasing free cash flow. Also, it shows the management’s confidence in the business outlook as they are aggressively raising payout.

When you combine high ROE with high recent dividend growth, you are not just buying quality or value. You are buying companies with quality momentum that are earning high returns, generating cash, and sharing more with shareholders.

What the Market Got Wrong About March 2025

The index reconstitutes annually and rebalances quarterly. The March 2025 reconstitution provides a perfect case study to test this quality momentum thesis. The market's reaction has been mostly negative, with some saying its bet on energy made the fund too cyclical, and some saying removing companies like Pfizer (PFE) was a mistake.

But look at these changes through the quality momentum framework, and you’ll see a completely different picture. The index was not making sector bets; it was simply looking for companies where solid cash flow currently exists.

It selected energy companies because after years of capital discipline post-2014, their business models have transformed. For e.g. the 5-year CAGR of dividends of ConocoPhillips (COP) is 13.5%. Its ROE, cash flow, and balance sheet also improved greatly over the last decade. The index saw these companies as quality businesses with accelerating shareholder returns. That resulting in 21% energy exposure is not a sectoral bet; rather, it's the direct outcome of its approach.

For those wondering what if the exposure increases further. As the Energy sector is cyclical and is directly correlated with commodity prices, this could negatively impact the performance of SCHD. But here’s the thing: the index rule says that no single sector can represent more than 25% of the index. So, I don't see concentration risk becoming a real issue.

Actually, it has already come down to around 19.5%, and based on current commodity prices, I expect the December 2025 rebalance to further decrease it to around 17-18%. That would bring its maximum sectoral weightings back to the historical range while maintaining quality momentum.

The removal of regional banks also wasn't a call on interest rates or recession risk. Their dividend growth rate has declined. Their quality momentum was turning negative. The index simply did its job by rotating away from decelerating momentum, regardless of the sector.

Pfizer's business was also decelerating. Its Covid vaccine revenues collapsed, loss of exclusivity ("LOE") wave was approaching, and dividend growth slowed. Compare this to Merck (MRK), which is also facing LOE, but its blockbuster drug Keytruda is still doing great, sales up 18% in FY’24, and has years of runway remaining. Their dividend increased by 5.4% in 2024 compared to only 2.4% for Pfizer.

Some might view this as a healthcare reshuffle, but again, the framework made a usual rotation from decelerating momentum to accelerating momentum.

Look, I believe in Pfizer’s transformation story, but the algorithm does not care about narratives. It bets on companies already showing quality momentum and reallocates capital into those high-quality high-cash-flow companies.

Why SCHD's Pain Is Actually An Opportunity

SCHD's recent performance has been rough. Year to date it has returned only 1.4% in total compared to SPDR S&P 500 ETF's (SPY) 13%. For the last 12 months it has returned -2.3%, and since 2023 it has significantly lagged the S&P 500 because of the AI-driven tech rally.

I know this underperformance might frustrate some investors but understanding why it's happening can help us recognize the investment opportunity.

As the index reconstitutes only once per year based on trailing data. This creates a structural timing challenge. During the 2023 reconstitution, the algorithm looked at 2022 data and saw energy with solid cash flows (thanks to the 2022 commodity spike), tech stocks beaten down during the 2022 bear market, and financials appearing less favorable after various banks collapsed in early 2023.

It made changes for that environment, but then, AI rally began. SCHD was now locked into a value heavy portfolio for 12 months while the market moved in a different direction.

Again, during the March 2025 reconstitution, it shifted towards energy and away from the financial sector. And this time, tariff and recession concerns made energy companies temporarily unloved.

This does not mean the methodology is flawed. It shows a disciplined, rebalancing process that does not follow price momentum. The question is, do you think fundamentals are going to matter again, or is this time really different?

You should know that this pattern is not new to SCHD. During the 2018-2019 late-cycle growth rally, it underperformed the S&P 500. But during the 2022 bear market, it did much better than both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq exactly because of its disciplined nature.

Its 12.4% average annual return since inception (roughly 15 yr) was not delivered by winning every quarter. It was done by buying quality companies at favorable prices and holding them through various cycles.

It is this feature of SCHD that leads to poor performance during speculative rallies because the methodology filters out narrative driven businesses.

For example, the market narrative for Palantir (PLTR) is defense tech disruptor, trades at a forward P/E of over 230x, and has given a return of nearly 180% in the last 12 months. But SCHD’s filter eliminates it immediately because of no dividend history.

The market might be right about its long-term potential. But for SCHD to include you, prove your business model works, prove you can return cash to shareholders, and then do it for 10 years.

Take NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) as another example. The market narrative for NVIDIA is an essential AI infra disruptor. It saw exceptional growth, and has been paying a little dividend since 2012. But initially SCHD filtered it out as it lacked a 10-year dividend history. When NVIDIA finally qualified for SCHD, its valuation already seemed to carry a high speculative premium, which might have triggered other fundamental filters to exclude it.

This filtering approach proved its worth during the 2022 bear market, which was like a stress test.

As both Nasdaq and S&P 500 saw huge drawdowns, SCHD was barely impacted. As other portfolios were down significantly, SCHD’s dividends were not only stable, but they grew at a double-digit rate. The filter did what it was designed to do: keep investors capital out of the speculative bets.

This makes SCHD a powerful tool for various types of portfolio construction that can help take risk elsewhere. For example, a meaningful allocation (20-25%) of your overall portfolio into SCHD can provide the defensive cushion and income to navigate through the volatility from thematic bets. So, if your AI thesis fails, you still have a meaningful compounding element in the portfolio.

Looking forward, SCHD's dividend growth rate seems to be well positioned to accelerate as energy cash flows compound and recent additions mature. Energy companies like ConocoPhillips and Chevron have already announced dividend increases. Based on this, I expect SCHD's dividend growth rate to increase from the current ~6% to 8-9% by 2026.

Importantly, if we enter a period of slower economic growth (benefits defensive positioning), persistent inflation (benefits energy and pricing power), market volatility (benefits low-beta, cash generating businesses), these could act as macro tailwinds for SCHD. Considering the overextended valuation of the S&P 500 and NASDAQ, SCHD seems to be well positioned for the next fundamentally driven cycle.

Valuation Analysis

Based on their P/E multiple, SCHD is trading at a ~30 percent discount to the SPY, and over a 32 percent discount to its peer, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation (VIG). As it picks companies with similar or higher ROE, accelerating fundamental momentum, solid balance sheets, and durability, this forms a good asymmetric return profile.

I expect the valuation gap to decrease over time as the market will ultimately appreciate the quality momentum like characteristics of the portfolio. The 3.8% yield, which is ~3.5 times that of the SPY’s 1.1%, should fully cover opportunity cost while waiting for the next cycle.

Why do I think this is the best time for long-term investors? Because during low cyclical sentiments, the investor becomes frustrated and underestimation occurs. It is currently trading near the midpoint of its 52-week range and nearly 10% below its all time high, which offers good entries to those investors having a longer-term horizon. The best moment to buy a contrarian, disciplined strategy is exactly when it has led to a short-term underachievement.

The current valuation discount is simply too extreme given the portfolio's quality characteristics. Assuming SCHD's P/E expands from ~17.5x to just 20x (still below SPY) over 2-3 years as quality recognition returns or because mean reversion occurs. And combining this with dividend growth of 7% annually with a yield of 3.8%, it results in annualized total returns of 15-18%.

In the worst-case scenario, even if P/E expansion doesn't happen and we only see dividend growth at a much slower rate of 4%, it is still an 8-10% annualized return. This is quite reasonable for a low-volatility strategy.

Although the worst case has possibility in the near term, especially if the AI rally persists or the energy prices drop drastically, the base case is much more favorable in terms of risk/reward for long-term investors.

Risks to Thesis

The primary risk to my Quality Momentum thesis is a prolonged speculative rally where valuations detach from fundamentals for an extended period, similar to the dot-com bubble. In that case, SCHD's discipline would become a performance drag. However, with the market now trading at historically high levels, the asymmetry seems to favor SCHD.

SCHD's approach takes time because it is designed to deliver performance over a multi-year cycle, not in a single quarter. Those investors who abandon the strategy during an AI driven rally may miss the next unavoidable rotation when fundamentals become decisive again.

Also, since the factors it considers for filtering are backward looking (e.g. 10-year dividend history), it can be slow to respond to new trends. So I don't expect it to be the first to own the next groundbreaking company. Investors should be okay with this conservative and gradual approach and understand that because of this, they will miss opportunities like NVIDIA.

Investor Takeaway

The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF is a low-cost, highly accessible quantitative strategy available to retail investors. It uses an unemotional, rules-based approach to identify stocks with Quality Momentum.

SCHD offers investors a rare opportunity to buy an advanced strategy at a very low valuation because of the market's current obsession with price momentum in tech. With SCHD you're buying a portfolio of proven winners that are getting stronger, just before their next recognition phase.

The 2 years of frustrating underperformance weren't random. It resulted from the disciplined approach: that annual reconstitution process being temporarily out of sync with a speculative market. For investors willing to look ahead, SCHD represents a compelling asymmetric opportunity because I believe that same discipline is what will drive outperformance when fundamentals reassert themselves.

I have a Buy rating on the SCHD ETF with an expected 15-18% annualized return in 2-3 years.

data.title: Novo's Worst Year Is Your Best Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

On the surface, Novo Nordisk (NVO) looks like a great "growth at a reasonable price" opportunity (aka GARP) because its fundamentals keep improving, and the stock price still can't find its bottom, updating new lows each quarter since mid-2024:

But the attractiveness of Novo Nordisk has been present for many months, and all attempts to buy the dip in this stock turned into a nightmare for value-seeking GARP investors. Political concerns over US drug tariffs, the persisting pricing pressure on NVO's key drug (Wegovy), and rising competition amid disappointing trial data for their next-generation obesity drug (CagriSema) led to a sharp cut in guidance and a change at the top management level, which added to the stock's ugly price action.

However, by normal pharma standards, Novo's growth is projected to be superior even after all the negatives in hand. Yes, their near‑monopoly hypergrowth in obesity with premium pricing is in the past, and that has caused the valuation reset, but I believe that reset has gone too far now.
